questions.question: The Byzantine Empire, which preserved Roman culture and law for over a thousand years after Rome's fall, finally collapsed in 1453 when which power conquered Constantinople?
questions.explanation: The Byzantine Empire ended on May 29, 1453, when Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II captured Constantinople. The city was renamed Istanbul and became the Ottoman capital. The fall is often cited as the end of the Middle Ages and triggered a migration of scholars to western Europe that fueled the Renaissance.
questions.memory_hook: Byzantine Empire ended 1453 — Ottomans took Constantinople (now Istanbul).

questions.question: The French Revolution began in which year?
questions.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille on July 14 — now celebrated as Bastille Day. It ended the absolute monarchy in France and established the principles of liberty, equality, and fraternity. The Revolution eventually gave rise to Napoleon Bonaparte, who declared himself Emperor in 1804.
questions.memory_hook: 1789 = French Revolution. Think: 1776 is American, 1789 is French. Three years after the American Revolution inspired a generation of French intellectuals.

questions.question: Which South American country contains approximately 60% of the Amazon Rainforest and is home to a river that discharges more freshwater into the ocean than the next seven largest rivers combined?
questions.explanation: About 60% of the Amazon Rainforest lies within Brazil, with the remainder spread across Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Bolivia, and other nations. The Amazon River discharges approximately 20% of all freshwater that flows into Earth's oceans. The rainforest is home to an estimated 10% of all species on Earth and plays a critical role in regulating global climate. The Amazon Basin covers 7 million square kilometers.
questions.memory_hook: Amazon: 60% in Brazil. River carries 20% of all ocean-bound freshwater — more than the next 7 largest rivers combined. 10% of Earth's species live here. The planet's largest climate regulator.

questions.question: Operation Overlord — the largest seaborne invasion in history — took place on June 6, 1944. By what name is this pivotal World War II event commonly known?
questions.explanation: D-Day (June 6, 1944) saw Allied forces land on five beaches in Normandy, France — code-named Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword. Over 156,000 troops crossed the English Channel. The invasion opened a Western Front that accelerated Nazi Germany's defeat.
questions.memory_hook: D-Day = June 6, 1944, Normandy beaches. Five beaches, 156,000 troops.

questions.question: The FIFA World Cup is held every how many years?
questions.explanation: The FIFA World Cup takes place every four years, with the host country chosen years in advance by FIFA. The first World Cup was held in 1930 in Uruguay. The tournament features 32 national teams (expanded to 48 starting in 2026) competing over about a month. Brazil has won the most titles with five championships.
questions.memory_hook: World Cup = every 4 years. Like the Olympics. Only 32 teams make it (soon 48). Brazil wins it most — 5 times. Argentina and Germany each have 4.

questions.question: In tennis, what is the term for winning a game without the opponent scoring a single point?
questions.explanation: A 'love game' in tennis is a game won without the opponent scoring any points (0 points = love). The term 'love' for zero in tennis likely comes from the French 'l'oeuf' (the egg), referring to the egg-shaped zero. A set won 6-0 is called a 'bagel' and winning 6-0, 6-0 is called a 'double bagel.'
questions.memory_hook: Love game = opponent scores zero. 'Love' = zero in tennis, from the French word for egg. A bagel (6-0 set) is a whole egg. A double bagel (6-0, 6-0) is total domination.

questions.question: Titanic (1997), directed by James Cameron, was the first film to gross over $1 billion worldwide. Who played Jack Dawson?
questions.explanation: Leonardo DiCaprio played Jack Dawson alongside Kate Winslet's Rose DeWitt Bukater in Titanic (1997). The film cost $200 million to make and grossed $2.2 billion worldwide, winning 11 Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Director. It held the record as the highest-grossing film ever until Cameron's own Avatar surpassed it in 2010.
questions.memory_hook: DiCaprio = Jack in Titanic. 11 Oscars, $2.2 billion. Cameron bet everything on a movie about a ship that sank. It became the highest-grossing film in history at the time.

questions.question: David Bowie performed under a flamboyant alien rock star alter ego from 1972 to 1973, retiring the character dramatically mid-tour. What was this persona called?
questions.explanation: Ziggy Stardust was David Bowie's androgynous alien rock star persona, introduced on the 1972 album 'The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars.' Bowie famously 'killed' Ziggy Stardust mid-concert at the Hammersmith Odeon in 1973, shocking his band, who didn't know in advance.
questions.memory_hook: Ziggy Stardust = Bowie's alien persona, 1972-73. He killed it onstage without warning.

questions.question: Which 1994 Quentin Tarantino film features a hitman duo, a Royale with Cheese, and a briefcase with a mysterious glow?
questions.explanation: Pulp Fiction (1994) is Quentin Tarantino's landmark film starring John Travolta, Samuel L. Jackson, Uma Thurman, and Bruce Willis. The non-linear narrative follows interconnected stories of crime and redemption. It revitalized Travolta's career, was nominated for 7 Academy Awards (winning Best Original Screenplay), and is frequently listed among the greatest films ever made.
questions.memory_hook: Pulp Fiction = Tarantino, 1994. Royale with Cheese = McDonald's in France. The briefcase glows but we never see what's inside. Tarantino's rule: the audience's imagination is better than any prop.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle, discovered by James Chadwick in 1932, has no electric charge and is found in the nucleus of every atom except the most common form of hydrogen?
questions.explanation: The neutron has no electric charge and, together with protons, forms the nucleus of atoms. Chadwick's 1932 discovery completed the basic model of the atom. Hydrogen-1 (protium) is the only stable atom with no neutrons in its nucleus. The number of neutrons in a nucleus determines the isotope — carbon-12 has 6 neutrons, carbon-14 has 8 (same element, different mass). Neutron bombardment also triggers nuclear fission in uranium and plutonium.
questions.memory_hook: Neutron: no charge, discovered 1932 by Chadwick. Completed the atom model. Same protons + different neutrons = isotopes. Neutron bombardment = nuclear fission.

questions.question: What is the name of the force that slows objects down when they rub against a surface?
questions.explanation: Friction is the force that resists the relative motion of surfaces in contact. It is caused by microscopic irregularities in surfaces catching on each other. Friction can be useful (it's what lets tires grip the road and lets you walk without sliding) or unwanted (it wastes energy as heat in engines). Friction doesn't exist in space in a vacuum.
questions.memory_hook: Friction = resistance between surfaces. Without it, you couldn't walk, tires couldn't grip, and you couldn't hold a pen. It's what makes the physical world usable — and also what makes engines inefficient.

questions.question: Fiji, an archipelago of about 330 islands in the South Pacific, is the largest economy in which sub-region?
questions.explanation: Fiji is the largest economy in Melanesia, the sub-region of the Pacific that includes Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, and New Caledonia. Fiji consists of 330 islands (about 110 inhabited) with roughly 900,000 people. Its population is ethnically diverse — about 57% iTaukei (indigenous Fijian, of Melanesian descent) and 37% Indo-Fijian (descendants of laborers brought by Britain from India in the 1800s). Fiji is known for rugby sevens, where it won an Olympic gold medal in 2016.
questions.memory_hook: Fiji: 330 Pacific islands, largest Melanesian economy. Two main groups: indigenous Fijians (Melanesian) and Indo-Fijians (descendants of British-imported Indian laborers). That colonial history created a complex ethnic mix that has caused political tensions. Also known for rugby sevens — Fiji punches well above its weight.
